top_categories.workout_context: 280 miles. Your legs will beg to quit after 120. This trains the power output you need from mile 130 onward.
47,572ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 280 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
At Haute Route Dolomites, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
47,572ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Haute Route Dolomites' demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
280 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
170ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 47,572ft of climbing on paved Alpine and Dolomite mountain pass roads does to your power file on race day.
Low-cadence force work for 47,572ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Haute Route Dolomites. Train it until it feels boring.
Haute Route Dolomites' paved Alpine and Dolomite mountain pass roads forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 280 miles.

race_overlay.nutrition: Ultra-distance fueling for 280 miles: 80–100g carbs/hour from mile 1 — don’t wait until you’re hungry. Practice your exact race-day nutrition in every long training ride. Carry backup calories. 280 miles burns 8,000–12,000+ calories — you cannot replace them all, but you must try.
race_overlay.altitude: Haute Route Dolomites with 47,572ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. 20-25C temperature swings between valley floors and pass summits within a single stage; Wet descents at altitude after summit finishes in rain or fog — reduced visibility and cold hands.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Endurance to prepare you for 280 miles of paved alpine and dolomite mountain pass roads in Cortina d'Ampezzo / Bormio, Dolomites, Italy.
